topic: I bought a self storage unit at auction and the previous renter left every photo of his life but none of the photos contain him
title: I Bought His Storage Unit and He Was Not in Any of His Own Photos | True Scary Story
description_intro: I bought a self storage unit at auction last winter. The previous renter, Daniel Voss, had stored forty seven albums and three plastic bins of loose photographs there for nineteen years. I have been cataloging them for six months. Nineteen hundred and twelve photographs so far. He is not in a single one of them.
description:
I bought a self storage unit at auction last winter. The previous renter, Daniel Voss, had stored forty seven albums and three plastic bins of loose photographs there for nineteen years. I have been cataloging them for six months. Nineteen hundred and twelve photographs so far. He is not in a single one of them.

The facility is on Industrial Boulevard, the part of town that used to be warehouses. A climate controlled unit, eight feet by ten feet. The manager remembers Daniel coming in every Tuesday morning for nineteen years for coffee in the lobby. She cannot describe his face. The photographs themselves go back to nineteen seventy three. The most recent are dated last August, two years after he stopped paying his rent.

There is a detail in one of the photographs that I have only told one person about. There is a hand reaching into a birthday cake from outside the frame. The hand has no shadow. Watch to the end. I am still going back to the facility on Tuesdays.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #storageunithorror
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: The man whose storage unit I bought at auction left every photo of his life inside it. He is not in any of them. I have been looking for nearly six months. There are forty seven albums and three plastic bins of loose prints. I have catalogued nineteen hundred and twelve photographs so far. Weddings, birthdays, vacations, holidays. His parents at the lake. His son's high school graduation. His daughter holding her first child. Everyone he knew is in those photos. None of them include him. I went to the facility expecting tools and furniture. I am still going back, because someone keeps adding new prints to the bins.

close shot of a hand turning a photo album page, the prints showing a family at a lake from behind, the photographer's shadow not visible on the ground, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: The facility is on Industrial Boulevard, in a part of town that used to be warehouses. Discount Self Storage. Open twenty four hours. Unit two seventeen. Eight by ten feet. Climate controlled, which is unusual for the price he had been paying. The previous renter's name on the lease was Daniel Voss. He had rented the unit for nineteen years before he stopped paying. The manager told me Daniel was a polite man who came by every Tuesday morning for coffee in the lobby. The manager never thought to wonder why. The photographs in unit two seventeen go back to nineteen seventy three. The most recent are dated last August.
image_prompts: wide exterior shot of a row of orange self storage units at dusk, one unit door open and lit from inside, no people, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a metal storage unit number plate showing 217 in faded paint, the door frame visible behind it, horizontal 16:9 framing
wide interior shot of a climate controlled storage facility hallway, fluorescent ceiling lights overhead, identical roll up doors lining both sides, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a signed rental agreement document on a wooden desk, the signature line clearly visible with a name written in cursive, dim office light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a corkboard at a storage facility manager's office with a photograph of the building from the seventies pinned to it, dim overhead light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: The first week I emptied the unit. I rented a van and drove it all back to my apartment. I covered the kitchen table with the albums and started cataloging them. My first thought was to find Daniel's family and return everything. There was no will. No surviving relatives on the lease paperwork. The facility manager could not tell me where Daniel had gone. I called the names I could read on the backs of the prints. Half of them were dead. The other half did not remember Daniel Voss. One woman said she had been at the wedding in the photograph I was describing. She did not remember anyone named Daniel attending.
image_prompts: wide interior shot of a small apartment kitchen with a wooden table completely covered in photo albums and loose prints, a cup of coffee at the edge, late afternoon light through a window, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a hand reaching into a plastic bin of loose photographs at a kitchen table, only the hand visible, dim lamp light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a list of handwritten names and phone numbers on a notepad on a desk, several names crossed out, a pen lying across the paper, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a smartphone screen showing an outgoing call to a contact labeled with a single name, dim ambient living room light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of the back of a photograph held in a hand showing handwritten ink dating from nineteen eighty four, a faded inscription mentioning a wedding, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: The wedding photograph from album three is where I first noticed. The bride is in white, at the altar of a small chapel. Her parents are visible on either side. Sixteen people in the frame. I counted twice because something felt wrong about the picture without my being able to say what. The bride was smiling at someone outside the frame. Eyes lifted, mouth half open, the way you smile at a person not at a camera. I checked the next photograph in the same series. Same chapel. Same bride. Different angle. She is smiling at a different empty patch of air. There is no groom in any of the album three wedding photographs.
image_prompts: close detail of a chapel wedding photograph showing a bride in white at an altar, parents on either side, the groom position vacant, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a hand counting figures in a wedding photograph with a pen tip pointing at the bride, the figures aligned along a row of pews, horizontal 16:9 framing
wide shot of a small wedding chapel interior from the nineteen eighties, wooden pews, family members seated, a bride standing alone at the altar, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a bride smiling and looking off to the side, her gaze directed at empty space beside her, vintage photographic quality, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of an album page showing four wedding photographs in plastic sleeves, all from the same ceremony, the groom position empty in each, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: My first explanation was the simple one. Daniel had been the photographer at his own wedding. Some people do that. The bride is your bride. You stay behind the camera. You miss yourself in the album. I told myself this for two weeks. I kept finding the same pattern in album after album. The seven year old girl at her birthday party blowing out candles, parents seated, an empty chair across the table. The family hiking trip with everyone smiling at a camera that no one was holding. I made peace with it. Daniel had been a man who lived behind his lens. He chose to record other people. There was something almost sweet about it.
image_prompts: close shot of a child's birthday party photograph showing a young girl blowing out candles, smiling parents seated around a kitchen table, one empty chair across from her, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a family hiking photograph showing four people on a forest trail smiling at the camera, no photographer's shadow visible on the dirt path, horizontal 16:9 framing

wide shot of an apartment living room at evening with photo albums stacked beside a couch, a person seated on the floor with a single album open on their lap, viewed from behind, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a kitchen counter with a coffee cup half full beside a closed photo album, a small handwritten note tucked under the cup edge, dim morning light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a vintage photograph of an empty wooden chair pulled out from a dining table, set for a family meal, no people visible at that seat, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: It was the photo booth strip that broke me. Four frames. A woman in two of them, laughing. A man in the other two beside her. The third position in the booth is empty in every frame. There should not be a third position. Then I found the mirror photographs. Bathroom mirrors. Department store dressing rooms. A car window reflection. In each, someone is photographing themselves. In each reflection there is no one holding the camera. I started flagging the photographs with sticky tabs. By the end of that night I had four hundred and twelve tabs. Daniel was never the photographer. Daniel was just absent.
image_prompts: close detail of a four panel photo booth strip showing a young couple, the third seat in the booth empty in each frame, slight overexposure, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a vintage bathroom mirror selfie style photograph with a small camera visible but no person holding it in the reflection, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a department store dressing room mirror photograph showing only clothing on a rack and a camera held by no visible person, horizontal 16:9 framing
wide shot of a kitchen table covered with photographs each marked with a small yellow sticky note tab, an open notebook with handwritten observations, dim lamp light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a car window reflection in a photograph showing dashboard and steering wheel but no driver, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I went back to the facility office. The manager had Daniel's rental file in a filing cabinet. His driver's license photocopy had been blacked out with permanent marker, by hand, only over the photograph. The signature was visible. The address was visible. The license number was visible. The face was a careful rectangle of black ink. I asked if anyone had ever seen Daniel in person. The manager said yes. Every Tuesday for nineteen years. I asked her to describe him. She paused. She said she could not. She remembered talking to him. The coffee. The way he laughed. She could not picture his face.
image_prompts: close detail of a paper rental file in a metal filing cabinet drawer, a tabbed manila folder labeled VOSS visible, dim fluorescent overhead light, horizontal 16:9 framing

close shot of a photocopied driver's license document on a wooden desk, the photograph area covered with a careful rectangle of black permanent marker, the signature and address legible, horizontal 16:9 framing
wide shot of a storage facility office desk with a clipboard, a coffee mug, and a logbook open on its surface, late afternoon light through a window, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a hand pointing at an entry in an old paper logbook with handwritten Tuesday morning visits over many years, dim desk lamp light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of an open visitor sign in book with a series of identical signatures by the same hand across many dates, no name printed beside any of them, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I started looking for any reflection in any photograph. A car bumper. A window. A polished tabletop. A glass of water on a Christmas dinner table. The Christmas glass is where it happened. The photograph is taken from above. The glass reflects the ceiling and the underside of the table. At the top of the reflection there is a fragment that should be a forehead and an eye. It is not. It is a smooth surface. The water reflects a shape where Daniel's face would be. The shape is featureless. I held the photograph next to my desk lamp for an hour. It is not damage. It is what was photographed.
image_prompts: close detail of a vintage Christmas dinner table photograph viewed from above, glasses of water visible, candles, the place setting of the photographer at the bottom of the frame, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a magnifying glass held over a printed photograph, the reflection in a glass of water enlarged, no clear face visible in the reflection, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a reflection in a polished black car bumper showing pavement and a hand holding a camera but no person attached, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of an old window pane reflection in a photograph showing a porch railing and trees but no figure where the photographer should stand, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a photograph held under a desk lamp, the surface of a glass of water in the photograph being scrutinized, dim home office light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I found a photograph dated last August. Daniel stopped paying his rental fee two years before that. The photograph is of a child's third birthday. The cake reads three. The family in it are people I have started to recognize. The bride from album three. The seven year old girl from album one, now older, holding her own daughter. I had been cataloging them for months. The chair across from the daughter is empty. The candles on the cake are unlit. There is a hand reaching from outside the frame to light them. The hand is featureless. It has no shadow on the cake. The next morning a new photograph was inside the storage unit door.
image_prompts: close detail of a child's birthday cake photograph with the number three on the icing, the candles unlit, a small girl seated in front, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a hand reaching into a birthday cake frame to light a candle, the hand featureless and casting no shadow on the cake, horizontal 16:9 framing

wide shot of a family birthday party in a living room from above, the photographer's perspective showing an empty chair across the table, a recent date stamp visible at the photograph margin, horizontal 16:9 framing

close shot of a storage unit interior at dawn, a single photograph lying on the concrete floor just inside the partially open door, no figures, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I rented a new unit at Discount Self Storage. Two eighteen. Next to Daniel's. I started taking photographs of my own life. Nine hundred prints in album one. Two hundred so far in album two. My wife at our front door. My son blowing out birthday candles. My parents at the lake. There are no photographs of me. I am not in any of them. The manager asks how I am every Tuesday morning. We have coffee. She has never described me to anyone. I asked her last week to try. She paused. She said she remembered the coffee. The way I laugh. I am going back this Tuesday.
